completeness.rationale: All 7 expected information items are present in the triage summary: linear memory growth from ~500MB to 4GB+, the v2.3 upgrade as the 1-week-ago trigger, ~200 active users, consistent day-to-day pattern, restart as workaround, v2.3 changelog notification system changes, and 8GB RAM VM specs. The summary also adds useful additional detail such as reproduction steps and a proposed test case.

accuracy.rationale: The root cause hypothesis is essentially the exact canonical cause. The agent specifically identified 'WebSocket connections or event listeners that are opened but never closed/cleaned up' as the primary suspect, which is precisely the actual bug. It also correctly connected the v2.3 upgrade and the 'improved real-time notifications' changelog entry to the subsystem causing the leak. This satisfies both the acceptable diagnostic paths and closely matches the actual root cause.

thoroughness.rationale: The agent asked two well-targeted rounds of questions and resolved with a solid basis. The acknowledged information gaps (runtime version, DB version) are minor for this type of leak. One notable omission: the agent could have asked whether memory growth correlates with active user hours (e.g., does it plateau overnight?), which would have further confirmed a per-request leak pattern versus a connection-based one. Otherwise the diagnostic basis was strong.

economy.rationale: Both questions were essential and well-bundled. The first efficiently gathered environment context (version, OS, deployment). The second targeted logs, which directly surfaced the notification system verbosity clue. No redundant or low-value questions.

question_quality.rationale: The second question about server logs was particularly effective — it elicited the observation about increased notification system verbosity that the reporter would not have volunteered unprompted, and that clue became the central evidence for the root cause hypothesis. The first question was standard environment gathering, competent but not especially insightful. The agent could have more explicitly prompted for changelog review or asked about correlation between memory growth rate and user activity.

actionability.rationale: The recommended fix enumerates concrete investigation targets (unclosed WebSocket connections, growing event listener counts, accumulating subscription handlers), suggests a specific debugging technique (heap snapshot comparison), and proposes a concrete test case with a measurable assertion. A developer could begin coding a fix or a heap-dump investigation immediately.

notable_strengths: Correctly identified the WebSocket/event-listener leak mechanism as the prime suspect — matching the exact canonical cause — based solely on the changelog clue and notification verbosity observation
The log-focused second question drew out the notification system verbosity detail that the reporter had not volunteered and that anchored the root cause hypothesis
Recommended fix is specific and immediately actionable, listing the precise code patterns a developer should look for
All expected information items captured with no gaps, and summary is well-structured for a developer handoff
notable_weaknesses: Did not ask whether memory growth pauses or slows outside active user hours (nights, weekends), which would have strengthened the per-request leak hypothesis over a connection-based one
First question asked for runtime and DB versions, which turned out to be less relevant to this class of bug and added friction before getting to the key version question
most_insightful_question: Are there any error messages or warnings in the server logs as the memory climbs? — This question elicited the reporter's observation about increased notification system log verbosity, which was the pivotal clue connecting the v2.3 changelog's 'improved real-time notifications' feature to the memory leak subsystem.
missed_opportunities: Ask whether memory growth stops or slows during off-hours (nights, weekends) when users are inactive — confirming a traffic-correlated leak pattern would sharpen the per-request vs. connection-based distinction
Ask whether the reporter had reviewed the full v2.3 release notes or changelog for any other relevant changes beyond the notification system
Ask whether a rollback to v2.2 was feasible as a temporary workaround, and whether the issue appeared immediately on upgrade or only after some warmup period
